title: Theoretical Evaluation of High-Permeability Wellbore Skin Effect on Aquifer Response under Pumping

research.question: What interface condition can represent a thin high-permeability wellbore skin without the physically inconsistent head jump produced by the conventional positive-skin formulation?
research.system: A constant-rate partially penetrating vertical well with wellbore storage in a vertically bounded anisotropic confined aquifer, surrounded by a very thin high-permeability skin zone.
research.inputData: Synthetic parameter scenarios evaluated with the proposed negative-skin-factor model, its large-time approximation, the conventional positive-skin model, and an existing two-zone solution.
research.method: Reduce a thin two-zone skin by Taylor expansion to an interface with continuous drawdown and discontinuous flux, solve the partially penetrating well problem by Laplace and finite cosine-Fourier transforms, invert numerically, and assess sensitivity.
research.benchmark: The negative-skin solution is compared with its large-time asymptote and the Pasandi et al. two-zone model; positive- and negative-skin responses are contrasted over wellbore and aquifer parameters.
research.uncertainty: Differential importance measures rank local parameter effects, but no field data are analyzed. The thin-skin reduction relies on negligible skin storage and spatially averaged wellbore drawdown assumptions stated in the derivation.
research.conclusion: A high-permeability skin is represented by continuous head and a flux jump associated with redistribution along the well interface; it affects intermediate and late drawdown, especially when skin-zone porosity increases abruptly.

narrative.throughline: Repair the interface physics for a highly permeable skin, carry the new flux jump through a partially penetrating well solution, and compare the resulting drawdown with a resolved skin zone.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A conventional skin factor adds a wellbore head loss while keeping radial flux continuous.
narrative.stakes: Extending that positive-skin rule to a high-permeability skin reverses the expected head ordering even though water still flows toward the well.
narrative.resolution: The new interface keeps drawdown continuous and lets flux change because water redistributes vertically within the thin conductive skin.

background.premise: The conventional positive-skin approach assumes discontinuous hydraulic heads and continuous flux across a low-permeability skin.
background.visibleEvidence: A sharp drawdown step appears at the well wall while equal flux arrows cross the interface.
background.whyNow: That construction is widely used because it compresses a skin zone into one lumped factor.
background.boundary: Its head-loss interpretation is physically appropriate only for positive low-permeability skin.
background.locator: PDF page 3, Introduction; PDF page 5, Equation 19
background.sourceExcerpt: approach assumes discontinuous hydraulic heads between the wellbore
background.visualObject: positive-skin well interface with a head step
background.motionPurpose: Establish the conventional continuity choices before reversing them.
background.stepDetail: Draw a head discontinuity at the well wall and pass equal radial flux arrows through it.
background.transitionBridge: Apply the same rule to a high-permeability skin and expose the contradiction.

background.premise: Applying positive-skin head loss to negative skin can place wellbore head above aquifer head despite inward pumping flow.
background.visibleEvidence: The hydraulic-head profile slopes the wrong way while the pumping arrow still points into the well.
background.whyNow: A high-permeability skin needs a different reduced interface law rather than a negative value inserted into the positive-skin equation.
background.boundary: The contradiction concerns a skin more permeable than the surrounding aquifer.
background.sourceExcerpt: in a physically impossible situation where the wellbore head exceeds
background.visualObject: inconsistent head profile beside an inward flow arrow
background.motionPurpose: Make the sign inconsistency immediate and physical.
background.stepDetail: Raise the wellbore head above the aquifer while retaining inward flow, then mark the contradiction.
background.transitionBridge: Resolve a thin conductive skin to derive a consistent interface.
background.minimalText: Negative PSF gives impossible head ordering

background.premise: The pumping well operates at a constant rate.
background.visibleEvidence: A fixed pumping arrow draws water into the well throughout the modeled test.
background.whyNow: The forcing must be fixed before the screen geometry and aquifer response are introduced.
background.boundary: The source model treats the pumping rate as constant over the analytical response.
background.sourceExcerpt: operates at a constant pumping rate ������ [L3T−1].
background.visualObject: Constant pumping arrow at the well
background.motionPurpose: Establish the source forcing as one independent premise.
background.stepDetail: Hold the pumping arrow at one fixed magnitude.
background.transitionBridge: The constant-rate well receives its finite screen interval in B04.

background.premise: The pumping well is partially penetrating.
background.visibleEvidence: The well screen occupies only a finite interval of the aquifer thickness.
background.whyNow: The finite screen creates vertical redistribution along the well interface.
background.boundary: Partial penetration is represented by the source's stated upper and lower screen elevations.
background.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1, model description and Figure 1
background.visualObject: Finite well-screen interval within the confined aquifer
background.motionPurpose: Own the partial-penetration geometry separately from material properties.
background.stepDetail: Reveal only the finite upper and lower screen endpoints.
background.transitionBridge: Radial and vertical conductivity directions are attached in B05.

background.premise: The aquifer has anisotropic hydraulic conductivity.
background.visibleEvidence: Radial and vertical conductivity arrows carry different labels and magnitudes.
background.whyNow: The transform solution must retain separate radial and vertical flow controls.
background.boundary: The source defines anisotropy between radial and vertical hydraulic conductivities.
background.visualObject: Distinct radial and vertical conductivity arrows
background.motionPurpose: Introduce anisotropy as its own source premise.
background.stepDetail: Attach separate conductivity labels to the radial and vertical arrows.
background.transitionBridge: The anisotropic field is enclosed by vertical no-flow boundaries in B06.

background.premise: The aquifer is vertically bounded by impermeable layers.
background.visibleEvidence: No-flow markers close the top and bottom of the aquifer cross-section.
background.whyNow: These vertical boundaries determine the finite cosine basis used later.
background.boundary: The no-flow condition applies to the aquifer top and bottom in the source model.
background.sourceExcerpt: The aquifer is bounded by two impermeable layers
background.visualObject: Impermeable top and bottom aquifer boundaries
background.motionPurpose: Close the vertical domain before the thin-skin reduction begins.
background.stepDetail: Place no-flow markers along the top and bottom boundaries.
background.transitionBridge: The bounded well geometry collapses into the thin-skin expansion in M01.

methods.input: Aquifer-side flux sampled across a very thin high-permeability annulus
methods.output: First-order approximation of aquifer flux at the skin surface
methods.validity: The expansion discards higher-order terms in the small skin thickness.
methods.sourceExcerpt: can be approximated using a first-order Taylor series expansion
methods.visualObject: Thin annulus magnified into two adjacent radial flux samples
methods.motionPurpose: Show only the small-thickness expansion at this step.
methods.stepDetail: Magnify the annulus; expand the outer flux about the skin surface; discard the higher-order remainder.
methods.transitionBridge: The skin continuity equation relates the flux difference in M02.

methods.output: Flux-difference relation containing skin storage and surface divergence
methods.validity: The continuity equation is evaluated within the thin skin zone.
methods.sourceExcerpt: continuity equation, expressed in terms of Darcy fluxes within
methods.visualObject: Expanded radial flux connected to storage and along-skin flux divergence
methods.motionPurpose: Introduce mass conservation as a separate physical operation.
methods.stepDetail: Insert the expanded flux into the skin continuity equation; retain storage and along-skin divergence terms.
methods.transitionBridge: Darcy relations convert fluxes to head gradients in M03.

methods.validity: The substitution uses the radial and along-skin Darcy relations defined by the source.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Based on Darcy’s Law, the fluxes
methods.visualObject: Flux arrows changing into radial and vertical head-gradient terms
methods.motionPurpose: Make the constitutive substitution distinct from mass conservation.
methods.stepDetail: Replace each flux with its Darcy head-gradient expression; retain the resulting interface relation.
methods.transitionBridge: The spatially averaged wellbore term is removed in M04.

methods.input: Head-based interface balance containing an along-interface derivative of wellbore drawdown
methods.output: Interface balance without the along-interface wellbore term
methods.validity: The source treats wellbore drawdown as spatially averaged and independent of distance at the interface.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, discussion following Equation (13)
methods.sourceExcerpt: wellbore drawdown is spatially averaged and independent of distance
methods.visualObject: Along-interface wellbore derivative fading from Equation 13
methods.motionPurpose: Own the spatial-averaging reduction independently of skin storage.
methods.stepDetail: Hold Equation 13 fixed and remove only the wellbore spatial-derivative term.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining skin-storage term is assessed separately in M05.

methods.input: Reduced interface balance retaining the skin-storage contribution
methods.output: Negative-skin interface relation driven by surface diffusion along the well wall
methods.validity: The source neglects skin storage because both skin thickness and skin specific storage are small.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, discussion following Equation (13) and Equation (14)
methods.sourceExcerpt: storage in the skin layer is very small
methods.visualObject: Skin-storage term fading from the reduced interface balance
methods.motionPurpose: Show the second source-stated reduction without removing surface redistribution.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the reduced balance fixed and remove only the small skin-storage contribution.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced interface receives the continuous-drawdown condition in M06.

methods.input: Aquifer drawdown and reduced negative-skin factor at the well wall.
methods.output: A continuous head interface with discontinuous radial flux caused by along-skin redistribution.
methods.validity: The condition represents high-permeability skin; it is not the conventional positive-skin head-loss condition.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Assuming continuity of drawdown between the skin zone and aquifer formation
methods.visualObject: continuous drawdown curve crossing the well wall with unequal flux arrows
methods.motionPurpose: Replace the physically inconsistent head jump with the paper's negative-skin interface.
methods.stepDetail: Join the head profile smoothly while splitting the radial flux arrows by the surface-diffusion contribution.
methods.transitionBridge: Embed the condition in the partially penetrating well problem.

methods.input: Dimensionless anisotropic confined-flow equations with partial-screen and negative-skin boundary conditions.
methods.output: Laplace-domain governing equations with transformed initial and boundary conditions.
methods.validity: Initial drawdown is zero, radial drawdown vanishes at infinity, and top and bottom boundaries are impermeable.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To obtain the solution to the proposed model, the Laplace transform
methods.visualObject: time-dependent cylindrical drawdown field mapped onto a Laplace-parameter axis
methods.motionPurpose: Remove explicit time dependence while retaining the radial and vertical coordinates.
methods.stepDetail: Map the time-dependent equations and their initial and boundary conditions into the Laplace domain.
methods.transitionBridge: Pass the Laplace-domain equations to the finite-depth transformation in M08.

methods.input: Laplace-domain governing equations with transformed initial and boundary conditions.
methods.output: Finite cosine-mode radial equations with transformed boundary conditions
methods.validity: The finite-depth transform is consistent with the impermeable top and bottom boundary conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the finite cos-Fourier transform is applied (Povstenko, 2015); that gives
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain drawdown field decomposed into unsolved vertical cosine modes
methods.motionPurpose: Represent bounded depth dependence before solving the transformed radial problem.
methods.stepDetail: Project the Laplace-domain depth dependence onto the finite cosine basis.
methods.transitionBridge: The transformed radial equations are solved in M09.

methods.input: Finite cosine-mode radial equations with transformed interface and far-field conditions
methods.validity: The modified-Bessel solution satisfies the transformed radial equation and its well and infinite-distance conditions.

methods.stepDetail: Apply the transformed well and far-field conditions and reveal the Equation 24 radial-mode solution.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved modes return to the depth coordinate in M10.

methods.validity: The finite inverse transform reconstructs the bounded vertical coordinate before time inversion.

methods.transitionBridge: The depth-resolved response returns to physical time in M11.

symbols.meaning: aquifer drawdown at radial distance r depth z and time t
symbols.transition: The field is evaluated at the well interface and transformed into modal components.
The pumping arrow forces the partial-screen boundary.
Anisotropy shapes the vertical cosine modes.

symbols.meaning: skin factor for the high-permeability interface
symbols.transition: The factor controls the discontinuous interface flux.

symbols.meaning: differential importance measure for parameter i
symbols.transition: The curves return attention to the parameters governing physical redistribution.

return.visualObject: the original partially penetrating well with a smooth head profile across the high-permeability skin and unequal radial flux arrows balanced by vertical interface redistribution
return.claim: The negative-skin interface provides a physically consistent thin-skin representation and reproduces the principal behavior of the resolved two-zone model in the tested scenarios.
return.claimBoundary: The result is theoretical and synthetic. The paper analyzes no field pumping test, and its thin-skin reduction neglects skin storage and along-interface variation of spatially averaged wellbore drawdown.
return.locator: PDF pages 8-9, Conclusions and discussion following Figure 7
return.sourceExcerpt: of this study is the lack of field data for analysis, which prevents

visual.primaryObject: partially penetrating pumping well in a vertically bounded anisotropic confined aquifer
visual.accentObject: thin high-permeability skin with continuous head and discontinuous flux
visual.geometryNotes: Use cylindrical r-z geometry, impermeable top and bottom, a finite screen interval, well radius r_w, and a magnified annular skin. Contrast PSF and NSF continuity choices without depicting field observations.
